basis.reasoning: Assessment reasoning: The field_description asks for a comprehensive engineering/release decision document including OTA/binary implications, policy constraints as of July 2026, a root-cause tree, an actionable release-acceptance matrix, telemetry, rollout/rollback procedures, and test criteria. The provided field_answer is an extensive, multi-section document that covers many of these aspects: OTA vs binary guidance, mDNS vs Tailscale discovery, network security configurations, policy references, a root-cause tree, a three-release architecture plan, an OTA-vs-binary decision matrix, rollout/telemetry/acceptance criteria, self-healing/watchdog concepts, and identity/deduplication schemas. It also includes a decision section (1–15) that aligns with what the field description requires. Given the length and depth, the answer appears to address all major facets expected by the field description, including external-policy considerations and concrete criteria. However, due to the provided excerpts being fragmented and primarily serving as background evidence rather than a direct mapping to every claim, some specific claims in the field_answer may not be explicitly verifiable from the excerpts alone. Thus, while the answer is thorough and matches the intent, its claims are not wholly supported by the excerpts. Therefore, completeness is true, but excerpt support is false.

excerpts:
Runtime versions and updates - Expo Documentation
The "appVersion" policy will increment the runtime version whenever the app version is incremented, but if you forget to bump the app version when changing the native runtime, then you'll have a runtime version mismatch.
citations.title: Runtime versions and updates - Expo Documentation

excerpts:
If you want to make incompatible updates extremely unlikely, at the cost of making it necessary to create builds more often, then you can use the `"fingerprint"` policy.
The `"appVersion"` policy will increment the runtime version whenever the app version is incremented, but if you forget to bump the app version when changing the native runtime, then you'll have a runtime version mismatch.
This will increment the runtime version whenever anything that may impact the native runtime changes
citations.title: modificationDate: December 05, 2025 title: Runtime versions and updates description: Learn about different runtime version policies and how they may suit your project.

excerpts:
How I Manage Versioning in Expo Apps
Mar 2, 2026 — " runtimeVersion": { "policy": "fingerprint " }. That means: Fingerprint changes → runtime changes; Runtime changes → new native build required

excerpts:
From Android 17 (API level 37) and higher, if no configuration has been defined for localhost, an implicit configuration is included. By default, this configuration does the following: Allows cleartext traffic.
If your app needs to connect to destinations using cleartext traffic (HTTP), you can opt in to supporting cleartext to those destinations. For example, you might want to allow your app to make insecure connections to `insecure.example.com` . ``` <?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?> <network-security-config> <domain-config cleartextTrafficPermitted="true"> <domain includeSubdomains="true">insecure.example.com</domain> </domain-config> </network-security-config> ``` If your app needs to opt in to cleartext traffic to any domain, set `cleartextTrafficPermitted="true"` in the `base-config` .
a numerical IP address and `InetAddress.isLoopback()` is `true` (for example, `127.0.0.1` or `[::1]` )
Starting with Android 9 (API level 28), cleartext support is disabled by default.
citations.title: Network security configuration  |  Security  |  Android Developers

tailscale-cgnat-keeper/docs/cgnat-explained.md at main ...
Carrier-Grade NAT (CGNAT) is an extra layer of NAT operated by your ISP. Instead of giving you a real public IPv4 address, your ISP assigns you an address from 100.64.0.0/10 (RFC 6598), and shares one real public IP among many subscribers.

excerpts:
NsdManager | API reference | Android Developers
Provides APIs for discovering and advertising network services on the local network. Network Service Discovery (NSD) allows applications to find other devices on a local network that support the services the application is interested in. For example, a game can find other players on the same local network, or a document viewer can find printers. The implementation is based on DNS Service
